name: What to Do After a Forklift Accident: A Guide for Injured Drivers and Bystanders

step.name: Step 1
step.text: What to Do After a Forklift Accident: A Guide for Injured Drivers and Bystanders

step.name: Step 2
step.text: A forklift accident is a chaotic event. Whether you were the operator, a pedestrian in a warehouse, or a worker on a loading dock, the moments following a collision or tip-over are critical for your health and your legal rights. Forklifts weigh thousands of pounds and often carry unstable loads, leading to catastrophic injuries that require immediate action.

step.name: Step 3
step.text: 1. Seek Immediate Medical Attention

step.name: Step 4
step.text: Your health is the first priority. Even if you feel 'fine' or just 'shaken up,' you must be evaluated by a medical professional. Adrenaline often masks serious internal injuries, spinal damage, or concussions. In Utah and Idaho, seeing a doctor immediately creates a formal record that the injury occurred at work, which is the foundation of any workers' compensation claim.

step.name: Step 5
step.text: 2. Report the Accident to Your Supervisor

step.name: Step 6
step.text: Do not wait until the next day. Report the incident to your direct supervisor or HR department immediately. Ensure that a written accident report is created. If you are incapacitated, have a trusted coworker notify management. In Idaho, you generally have 60 days to report, but waiting even a week can lead the insurance company to question whether the accident actually happened at work.

step.name: Step 7
step.text: 3. Document the Scene and Equipment

step.name: Step 8
step.text: If you are physically able, or if you can have a coworker assist, take photographs of the scene. Specifically, photograph:

step.name: Step 9
step.text: <ul>

step.name: Step 10
step.text: • The forklift involved (including the model number and any visible damage).

step.name: Step 11
step.text: • The load that was being carried.

step.name: Step 12
step.text: • Floor conditions (grease, debris, or lack of safety markings).

step.name: Step 13
step.text: • Any obstructed views or broken mirrors on the machine.

step.name: Step 14
Step 15
step.text: This evidence is often cleaned up or repaired within hours of an accident, so early documentation is vital.

step.name: Step 16
step.text: 4. Identify Witnesses

step.name: Step 17
step.text: Warehouse and construction sites are busy. Get the names and contact information of anyone who saw the accident or the conditions leading up to it. Coworkers may be hesitant to speak later for fear of retaliation, so getting their account early is crucial for your attorney.

step.name: Step 18
step.text: 5. Ask About the Forklift’s Maintenance History

step.name: Step 19
step.text: Many forklift accidents are caused by mechanical failure—bad brakes, faulty hydraulics, or worn tires. Your attorney will need to subpoena the maintenance logs for that specific machine to see if the employer was neglecting safety standards. If the equipment was defective, you may have a 'third-party claim' against the manufacturer in addition to your workers' comp claim.

step.name: Step 20
step.text: 6. Consult a Workers' Compensation Specialist

step.name: Step 21
step.text: Forklift accidents often result in high-value claims because the injuries are severe. Insurance companies often try to blame the operator to avoid paying. An attorney experienced in Utah and Idaho workers' comp law can protect you from these tactics. At Just Work Comp Law, we help injured workers navigate the complexities of these claims with no upfront costs. Call 801-746-0290 to discuss your case.

description: What to Do After a Forklift Accident: A Guide for Injured Drivers and Bystanders - Riverton
